question_text: A satellite orbits Earth in a circular path. Which statement best explains why it doesn't fall straight down to Earth?

options.label: A
options.text: Gravity doesn't act on objects in space

options.label: B
options.text: The satellite's sideways velocity means it constantly falls towards Earth but misses it, creating a circular orbit

options.label: C
options.text: The satellite is held up by atmospheric pressure

options.label: D
options.text: The satellite has no weight in orbit

correct_answer: The satellite's sideways velocity means it constantly falls towards Earth but misses it, creating a circular orbit
explanation: Gravity does act on satellites, constantly pulling them towards Earth. However, satellites have horizontal velocity. Gravity changes the direction of this velocity without stopping it, causing the satellite to follow a curved path around Earth—it's always falling but always missing. This balance creates a stable orbit.
